segments.s461-g89: به گرد لب آب is a prepositional phrase meaning “around the edge of the water” (به + گرد = around; لب آب = ‘brink/edge of the water’ with لب governing the genitive آب). لشکر is the direct object/subject (here ‘the army/host’) and کشید is a simple past of کشیدن used in a literary/poetic sense “to draw up” or “to march/position” (verb after its object/phrase is a common poetic word order). Note that کشید here is the motion/deployment sense, not the agricultural senses listed in the lemma entry.
segments.s461-g90: ز introduces cause/means here (“because of” / “from”), جوشن = ‘cuirass/armor’; the clause ز جوشن ... ندید therefore reads “because of their armor … did not see.” کسی with the negative verb ندید yields the indefinite negative sense “no one” (کسی ... ندید = nobody saw). آب دریا is a genitive construction ‘the water of the sea’ (i.e. the sea), and ندید is past simple negated (“did not see”), with the subject omitted/understood from context.
segments.s461-g73: Noun phrase with the head noun كمرهای (plural) followed by its adjective زرین; the conjunction و links to a parallel phrase زرین ستام. The repetition of زرین is a poetic emphatic device (golden, and even ‘gold‑praised’/gold‑adorned). ستام is an archaic/poetic word (here used attributively) and the whole phrase lists ornamented waist‑bands/girdles.
segments.s461-g74: Demonstrative همان ('that/the same') precedes the noun تیغ (sword); the adjective هندی follows the noun as is normal in Persian poetry. The prepositional phrase به زرین نیام attaches to تیغ and indicates the sword’s sheath (به used here in the sense 'in/into' or 'with'); نیام is the scabbard. Word order and particles are poetic but straightforward: 'that sword (Indian) in a golden scabbard.'
segments.s461-g75: Prepositional/participle phrase introduced by the poetic preposition ز 'from/of' followed by the coordinated objects دیبا و دینار (brocade and dinars). چنان is an adverb 'so much/to that extent' and بیافت is a past passive/impersonal verb meaning 'fell/occurred/were bestowed' (poetic usage meaning many items came to hand).
segments.s461-g76: The conjunction که introduces the result clause. از + خواسته gives the cause/source 'because of the request/wish'; بارگی is a poetic noun here meaning the burden/weight or the burden‑bearing (the demand that must be borne). برنتافت is a past negative/ability verb 'did not/could not bear' (literally 'did not endure'). Together: '...so much … that he could not endure the burden of the request.'
segments.s461-g77: بسی is an adverb meaning 'many/much' or 'a great deal' and modifies زینهاری (warnings/exhortations). بیامد is the past tense 'came'. Syntactically this segment states that many warnings/announcements arrived; the following noun سوار (rider) resumes identification of who came.
segments.s461-g78: A coordinated nominal phrase in apposition to سوار: بزرگان (the great ones/leaders) specified as جنگاور (warriors) and نامدار (renowned). The adjectives follow their nouns in the normal Persian poetic order and list the qualities of the arriving horsemen.
segments.s461-g79: وزان is a compound adverbial 'and/from that (place/time)'; جایگه 'place/position' is the object and ساز here is used in the general/poetic sense 'means/device/establishment' (not merely musical instrument). ایران is the subject and گرفت is the past tense 'took/seized'. The clause means 'from that point Iran secured/took the position.'
segments.s461-g80: Two coordinated noun phrases (دل شیر and چنگ دلیران) are objects of the repeated verb گرفت; دل شیر literally 'the heart of the lion' (a stock metaphor for courage) and چنگ دلیران 'the harp (metaphorically the affection or fame) of the brave'. The repeated گرفت ties these captures back to Iran as subject in the previous segment; the construction is emphatic and metaphorical.
segments.s461-g25: This clause literally places 'goodness' within the king's reach: the preposition به + noun نیکی ('goodness') precedes the copula بود ('was/was found'), and then the possessed noun phrase شاه را دست‌رس ('the king’s access/hand‑reach') follows. را marks شاه as the definite/ topical patient of دست‌رس; the overall order is [to/for + thing] + [copula] + [possessor + را + noun], a poetic word order where the possessed notion appears after the verb.
segments.s461-g26: This short clause gives the contrasting circumstance. به بد روز گیتی ('on/at an evil day of the world') is a prepositional/temporal phrase (به + adjective + noun + modifier), followed by نجستست کس, an elliptic past form; here نجستست is given in the gloss as 'was defiled' and کس is an indefinite 'someone/anyone'. The phrase therefore functions as a single temporal adjunct + comment; the verb-like element appears at the end, as is normal in Persian poetic style.
segments.s461-g27: سکندر شنید ('Alexander heard') is a simple subject + past verb (subject before verb, common in epic diction). این پسند آمدش is an idiom: این پسند آمدش literally 'this liking came to him' = 'this pleased him'; آمدش is آمد + enclitic ش (to him), with the whole clause following the verb شنید to express consequence/response.
segments.s461-g28: سخن‌گوی را فرمند آمدش places the object (سخن‌گوی with را) before the verb-form which here is a past/reporting/command verb فرمند plus آمدش ('came to him'). فرمند in this context functions as 'he commanded / caused to be sent' and آمدش reports that the spokesman arrived; آمدش again combines آمد + ش 'to him' as a light verb phrase.
segments.s461-g29: به فرمان او ('by his command') is a prepositional agent phrase placed before the main clause. کرد کاری که کرد is a repetitive, emphatic construction: کرد ('he did') + کاری ('a deed/work') + که کرد ('that he did'), where the relative/connector که introduces the clause repeating or specifying the action. The verb کرد appears twice for emphasis; word order is typical Persian (adjunct — object — verb) though poetic repetition intensifies the meaning.
segments.s461-g30: ز بزم و ز رزم و ز ننگ و نبرد is a coordinated list introduced by the poetic preposition ز (short form of از, 'from'/'in/with') repeated before each noun for rhetorical effect. The nouns are paired and contrasted (feast/banquet — battle; shame — combat). No finite verb appears here because the list modifies or specifies the kinds of actions referenced in the previous clause.
segments.s461-g31: به نو هر زمانیش بنواختی is an adverbial/verb phrase: به نو ('anew') modifies بنواختی ('struck/played/composed'). هر زمانیش ('at every time/occasion') is a temporal modifier attached to the verb. Note the poetic past form بنواختی — in Ferdowsi's diction verbs often appear in older past endings; the subject is understood from context (the king).
segments.s461-g32: چو رفتی بر تخت بنشاختی begins with چو ('when'), a subordinating conjunction, followed by رفتی ('he went' — past tense) as the temporal clause, and the main clause بر تخت بنشاختی ('he set/established upon the throne'). بنشاختی is a past verb ('set up/established') and بر تخت ('upon the throne') is its locative complement; again the subject is implicit and verbs use epic/older endings.
segments.s461-g57: برون آمد is a past-tense verbal phrase (verb before subject here) meaning 'came out' or 'stepped forth'. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the', and نامور شهریار is a noun phrase 'renowned ruler/king' (نامور = 'renowned' modifying شهریار = 'king').
segments.s461-g58: This small phrase is appositional: بره‌بر is an epithet 'lamb-bearer' (used as a descriptive noun), چنان is a demonstrative/quantifier 'such' and لشکر نامدار = 'renowned army' (لشکر 'army' + نامدار 'renowned'). The items are juxtaposed (typical in epic Persian) rather than linked by an overt conjunction.
segments.s461-g59: درفشی here names the banner/standard as the subject. پس پشت (literally 'after/behind + back') functions as a locative phrase 'at the rear of', so پسِ پشت سالار روم = 'behind the commander of Rome' (سالار روم = 'the Roman commander').
segments.s461-g60: نوشته is the noun 'writing/inscription'. برو here is an archaic/fused form used with بر (effectively 'on it' or 'upon the [cloth]') rather than the simple verb 'go' (so its usual lemma senses don't fit directly). سرخ و پیروزه بوم describes the banner's field: 'a red and turquoise cloth/canvas' (بوم = cloth/canvas or field of the banner).
segments.s461-g61: همای is the Homa (a mythical royal bird) as topic. از is the preposition 'from' (here introducing the location on the object), and برو again is an older/fused locative element indicating the upper part or 'upon it' (not the modern verb 'go'). خیزرانش is 'his/its reed' (خیزران + enclitic -ش) and قضیب is an appositive 'rod/staff' specifying the object.
segments.s461-g62: نوشته is the inscription/text. بر او is the locative 'on it', and the following بر introduces the wording: برِ محبِ صلیب = literally 'upon: "lover/devotee of the Cross"' (محب صلیب is a noun phrase meaning 'lover/devotee of the Cross'). The sequence reports that those words were written on the staff or banner.
segments.s461-g63: به مصر آمد is a past-tense clause 'came to Egypt' (به + destination + آمد). از روم gives the source 'from Rome'. چندان سپاه is a quantifying noun phrase 'so many troops' / 'so great a host' (چندان = 'to that extent; so much', سپاه = 'army/host').
segments.s461-g64: که introduces a result clause 'so that/that', and بستند is past tense 'they closed' or 'they blocked'. بر مور و بر پشه راه literally lists small creatures 'on ants and on gnats' with راه 'road' at the end: idiomatically 'they shut the road even for ants and gnats' (i.e. the way was completely blocked).
segments.s461-g65: دو لشکر is the subject ('two armies'). به + روی + اندر آورده is a light-result phrase: اندرآوردن (here in participial/past form آورده) = 'to bring into (a state)', so به روی اندر آورده روی is a poetic way of saying 'brought (their) faces to (one another)' — i.e. 'came face to face.' The final روی echoes the idea of 'face' for emphasis; word order (noun + prepositional/result phrase) is typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s461-g66: ببودند is an archaic/poetic past copula (they were). یک هفته is a duration adverbial 'for a week' placed before the adjective (پرخاشجوی). The adjective follows (standard Persian order for predicate adjectives after a copula), so the clause means 'they were quarrelsome/aggressive for a week.'
segments.s461-g67: به هشتم is a temporal phrase 'on the eighth (day)'. به مصر اندر آمد is a locative/result clause 'came into Egypt' (اندر آمد = 'came/entered' in poetic register). شکست is the noun 'defeat' functioning as the thing that 'came' (the grammatical patient/theme placed with the verb in inverted/poetic order).
segments.s461-g68: سکندر is the subject (Alexander). سر راه ایشان is a locative expression 'at/on their road' (سر راه = 'head/along the road' + ایشان 'their/them'), and ببست is the past verb 'he closed/blocked.' Word order is Subject + locative phrase + verb, a normal Persian clause order.
segments.s461-g69: ز یک راه is a prepositional phrase 'from/at one road' (ز here = 'from/of' in poetic usage). چندان is an adverb 'so/to that extent' modifying گرفتار شد ('became trapped'); گرفتار is a predicate adjective/participial form 'trapped/caught', and شد is the past auxiliary 'became/was'.
segments.s461-g70: که introduces the result clause 'so that'. گیرنده را here names the agent/actor ('the captor' or 'the one who would take'), and را marks that noun (in poetic Persian را can mark the definite nominal as a theme/possessor here). دست بیکار شد is a predicative construction 'the hand became idle' (دست = 'hand/power', بیکار = 'idle', شد = 'became'). Together: 'so that the captor's hand became idle.'
segments.s461-g71: This is a poetic series introduced by prepositions: ز گوپال و از اسپ و برگستوان lists items 'from the cowherds and from the steeds and برگستوان.' Both ز and از function as 'from/of' in rapid enumeration, with و linking items. برگستوان here is another nominal item in the list; the whole phrase is a catalogue of sources/objects.
segments.s461-g72: This continues the list: ز خفتان (from sleepers/those asleep — خفتان is a verbal/noun form related to sleeping) وز (a poetic connective here joining the next item) خنجر هندوان ('the dagger(s) of the Indians' — خنجر = 'dagger', هندوان = 'Indians' used adjectivally). Grammatically it is another sequence of nouns introduced by ز and linked by the connector.
segments.s461-g81: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. بشنید is simple past 'he heard' with the subject name دارا following it (classical word order allows verb before subject). که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the reported content; لشکر ز روم is a noun phrase 'an army from Rome' where ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and روم names Byzantium/Rome.
segments.s461-g82: بجنبید and آمد are two past actions attributed to that army: بجنبید (3rd pl. past) = 'they moved/advanced', followed by the coordinating و 'and', then آمد 'came/arrived'. برین is an archaic/dialectal demonstrative 'this' (بر + ین) governing مرز و بوم 'frontier and land' (مرز = border, بوم = homeland). The verbs agree with لشکر (the army) mentioned in the previous clause.
segments.s461-g83: برفتند is past 3rd plural 'they departed/advanced'. ز اصطخر is a prepositional phrase 'from Istakhr' (اصطخر here a place name). چندان directly modifies سپاه and means 'so many / such a great number of' (i.e., 'a host so large'), with سپاه 'army, host' the noun being quantified.
segments.s461-g84: که introduces a result clause ('so that / such that'). از نیزه is instrumental/associative 'with spears' or 'by means of spears'. بر here means 'against/on' and باد literally 'wind' — together the phrase is poetic; بستند راه is the verbal phrase 'they closed the way/road' (بستند = closed, راه = way). The overall sense: the host was so numerous that, with their spears, they closed the road.
segments.s461-g85: همی is an older continuative/adverbial particle (here marking ongoing action, 'was continually/was in the act of'). داشت functions as a past progressive auxiliary meaning 'was having/was making' so that همی داشت ... آهنگ = 'he was bent on / was preparing an intention'. از پارس denotes origin/location 'from Pars', and آهنگ روم is 'the intention/aim toward Rome' (آهنگ = intention, روم = Rome).
segments.s461-g86: کز is a fused classical particle here introducing the subordinate clause ('that/from which' or 'so that'), with ایران as the source 'from Iran'. گذارد is used in a sense of 'pass, go across' (classical usage meaning 'to pass/leave') and takes به + noun for direction: به آباد بوم = 'into the prosperous/inhabited land' (آباد modifying بوم). The clause expresses intended movement from Iran toward a flourishing territory.
segments.s461-g87: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آورد is past 'he brought/led' (here 'he brought/led the army'). لشکر is the direct object 'the army' and به پیش فرات is the prepositional phrase 'to/before the Euphrates' (پیش = before/in front of, فرات = the Euphrates).
segments.s461-g88: سپه را uses را as a topical/definite marker ('as for the host'); عدد بود is a copular construction 'the number/quantity was'. بیش از is the comparative 'more than', and نبات ('plants/vegetation') is used hyperbolically to indicate an immense number. Together: 'the host's number was more than vegetation' (i.e., innumerable).
segments.s461-g9: به پیش + proper name + شد + noun phrase: the prepositional phrase به پیش (into the presence of) precedes the verb; شد is the past form of شدن used here intransitively as 'came/arrived' (poetic word order places the subject آن پاک‌رای after the verb). پاک‌رای is an archaic/epithet compound meaning a man of pure/virtuous reputation.
segments.s461-g10: زبان کرد = 'made speech' (an idiomatic periphrasis: noun + کردن = to speak); گویا here modifies کرد as 'eloquently' (an adjectival/adverbial use). و links the clauses; بگرفت جای = past tense (بـ+گرفت) 'took' + جای 'place/seat' — a simple sequence of actions. The verb کردن is acting as a light verb creating the verbal sense 'spoke'.
segments.s461-g11: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت here marks speech — poetic order places the indirect object before گفت). کای is the vocative 'O', followed by titles/adjectives مهتر 'chief' and شادکام 'fortunate' addressing the addressee.
segments.s461-g12: همی is an emphatic/continuous particle ('indeed/ever' or 'surely' here); گم is an adjective 'lost' (used with کردن to mean 'to lose'); کنی is 2nd person sg. present/subjunctive form of کردن used here to express what the addressee will do (English: 'you will lose'). اندرین کار = 'in this affair' (اندرین = 'in this'); نام = 'name' meaning reputation/fame — object of گم کنی.
segments.s461-g13: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'for/that'; تخت کیان is a genitive 'throne of the Kayanids' (noun + proper collective); چون تو 'such as you' (چون = like); بسیار modifies دید 'has seen many/often seen' — دید is simple past 'saw/has seen' in narrative register.
segments.s461-g14: نخواهد + همی + با کسی + آرمید: نہخواهد here is modal/future negative 'will not (be)' or 'will not allow'; همی gives a continuous/ever sense ('ever/indeed'); با کسی 'with anyone'; آرمید is past form meaning 'rest' but here used in the sense 'will not rest/settle' — overall: 'it will not rest with anyone.' The verb is in classical/past form used idiomatically for future negation.
segments.s461-g15: هرانگه که = 'whenever/at that time when'; گویی here is the speech/quotative or concessive marker 'as you say' (classical usage where the verb 'to say' appears in subordinate sense); رسیدم به جای = 'I have reached/attained a place' (رسیدم past tense first person or perfect sense). The clause sets a hypothetical/temporal condition.
segments.s461-g16: نباید is an impersonal modal 'should not / must not'; به گیتی 'by/the world' (agent or means); مرا+را = 'me' with the object marker (here segmented as م + را in the edition); رهنمای is 'guide' (noun or verbal noun used as predicate) — the construction says the world ought not to guide me / the world should not be my guide.
segments.s461-g49: مرا (here split as م + را) functions as the definite/accusative object (را marks the object). رویِ گیتی is a genitival/locative phrase 'place/face on the world'. بباید is the modal 'must/ought' and is followed by the past-stem سپرد used as the lexical verb ('to hand over/abandon') — together giving an obligation ('must surrender/leave'). Word order is object/locative phrase before the modal + verb, a typical classical Persian arrangement where the modal precedes the lexical verb.
segments.s461-g50: بد و نیک is a simple coordinate noun phrase 'the bad and the good'. چندی here means 'how much/how many' (an interrogative/quantifying sense). بباید + شمرد again gives an obligation: 'one must count/reckon' — شمرد is the past stem used after the modal to express the action. The clause therefore states the necessity to reckon the quantities/amounts of bad and good.
segments.s461-g51: شما را is a topical/accusative phrase 'you' with the object marker را (here marking the person affected). بباید is the modal 'must' and کنون is an adverb 'now' modifying the following infinitive ساختن ('to make/build'). The infinitive ساختن functions as the complement of بباید, so the clause means 'you must now cause/make...'.
segments.s461-g52: دل is the object of ساختن ('make the heart'). از + بوم indicates separation/source 'from the homeland'. و links the second coordinated action آرام پرداختن: آرام (calm/peace) + پرداختن here means 'to give oneself to / attend to / devote oneself to' — together 'to leave the homeland and devote the heart to rest/peace'. Grammatically, an infinitive phrase (ساختن ... پرداختن) gives two coordinated outcomes for the object دل.
segments.s461-g53: سر + گنجهای نیا is a compound meaning something like 'the top/lids of the ancestors' treasures' (سر = top/head, here 'lid/top'). باز کرد is the simple past 'opened' (کرد = did/made used with باز as 'opened'). Subject is omitted (he), as is common in narrative Persian.
segments.s461-g54: بفرمود is an honorific past 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that') and is followed by لشکرش (his army) and ساز کرد ('made/put in order' — ساز here denotes preparation/means and کرد is the past 'did'), giving 'he ordered that his army be prepared.' The verb-final order in the subordinate clause is standard.
segments.s461-g55: به شبگیر is a temporal/locative phrase 'at nightfall / by night'. برخاست is the simple past 'he rose / departed'. از روم غو places the source after the verb in poetic diction: 'an uproar from Rome' (غو = tumult/uproar). The clause mixes temporal phrase + past action + a following noun of result/source, a common classical poetic order.
segments.s461-g56: ز شهر و ز درگاه سالار نو repeats the preposition ز ('from' in poetic register) for emphasis: 'from the city and from the court/threshold of the new commander.' درگاه سالار نو is a genitive construction 'the court of the new commander.' The doubled ز stresses the multiple sources of the uproar.
segments.s461-g33: چنان بد که is a result/continuative phrase: چنان ('such/so') + بد ('bad' in literal sense but here idiomatically part of 'so it was that...') introduces the following subordinate clause with که. روزی is an adverbial 'one day / it came to pass' (poetic), and فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite nominative 'a dispatched one' = 'a messenger' (the -ای marking indefiniteness).
segments.s461-g34: This is a string of attributive descriptions of the messenger. سخن‌گو and روشن‌دل are adjectival/nominal compounds describing manner and mind ('eloquent' and 'bright‑minded'); و links them; آزاده‌ای is a descriptive indefinite noun/adjective 'a free/noble‑hearted man' (the ending -ای again gives an indefinite flavor). The order is modifier + head, typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s461-g35: ز نزدیک is a prepositional/locative phrase 'from nearby'. دارا here is used as a proper name (from the root meaning 'possessor') and functions as the subject; بیامد is simple past 'came'. به روم marks the destination 'to Rome'. Word order places the locative phrase before the subject and verb for poetic effect.
segments.s461-g36: This is an interrogative clause. کجا 'where' fronted, باژ 'cry' is the noun, خواهد is the modal/future 'will (there be/come)', and ز آباد بوم is the source phrase 'from the prosperous homeland' (ز = from, آباد = prosperous, بوم = homeland). The verb خواهد gives the clause its prospective sense.
segments.s461-g37: به پیش سکندر is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of Alexander' (به + پیش = 'before/in front of'); بگفت is the past verb 'he said/told' and آن سخن is the direct object 'that speech' following the verb in this poetic ordering.
segments.s461-g38: This clause inverts normal prose word order for emphasis: غمی شد سکندر literally 'a grief became Alexander' — i.e., 'Alexander became sorrowful.' ز باژ کهن ('because of/from the ancient cry') gives the cause. شد is the past of شدن used as a copular/perfective verb 'became.'
segments.s461-g39: بدو is an indirect object 'to him'; گفت is the reporting verb 'he said.' What follows is reported imperative: رو (imperative 'go' / 'turn') + پیش دارا ('to/into the presence of Dārā') + بگوی (present‑imperative/jussive 'tell' or 'say to him'). The sequence marks a reported command.
segments.s461-g40: که introduces the content of the message 'that...'. از باژ ما 'from our cry' is the source phrase; شد کنون is a verbal clause 'has now become/has now arisen' (شد as perfective/copular with adverb کنون); رنگ و بوی is the compound predicate 'color and scent' (two nouns joined by و). The clause reports the effect/result attributed to 'our cry.'
segments.s461-g41: The initial که is a connective/relative 'that' linking this clause to what came before; مرغی is an indefinite noun 'a bird'. The second که is a relative 'which/that'. زرین is an adjective 'golden' modifying the noun that follows, همی is the archaic continuous/habitual marker (common in epic Persian) and کرد is the past/transitive verb 'made/did' with خایه (here a noun 'testicle') as its object. Word order is typical older Persian: adjective + aspect marker + object + verb.
segments.s461-g42: به مرد marks an indirect object 'to/for the man' (به + noun). The conjunction و links the two coordinated predications: the giving (خایه کردن به مرد) and a second action. سر باژ is a compact genitive-like phrase (ezāfe omitted poetically) meaning 'the head/chief of the cry/sound'; بی‌مایه is a predicate adjective 'devoid of substance/worthless'; کرد is the repeating past verb 'made/caused'.
segments.s461-g43: فرستاد is the past tense 'he sent' and پاسخ is its direct object 'an answer'. بدان سان is an adverbial phrase 'in that way/in that manner' formed from the element ب- plus دان + سان (poetic compounding), and شنید is the past 'he heard' (i.e. he heard/learned of it). The sequence states sending a reply and then someone hearing it.
segments.s461-g44: بترسید is the past 'he became afraid' (3rd person). وز here is an older/prepositional form equivalent to از 'from' (poetic usage) and روم is the place 'Rum/Byzantium'; شد is the auxiliary/past 'became' and ناپدید is a predicate/adjective 'vanished, gone'. Together they say that, on hearing it, he grew afraid and disappeared from Rum.
segments.s461-g45: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. سپه را uses the object marker را to mark 'the army' as definite. سراسر 'throughout/entire' modifies the object to mean 'the whole army', and بخواند is an older/habitual past form of خواندن used here as 'he called/summoned' (in epic language). The order (object modifier before verb) is normal in classical verse.
segments.s461-g46: گذشته سخن is a noun phrase 'the speech/tale of the past' with the adjective/attributive element before the noun. پیش ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'before them/in their presence' (پیش + pronoun), and براند is a verbal form from راندن literally 'he drove/put forth' but here used idiomatically to mean 'he brought forth/uttered' — i.e. he delivered the tale to the assembled army. Poetic compression sometimes uses راندن for 'set forward/speak'.
segments.s461-g47: چنین گفت is 'he said thus/so'. کز is the contracted classical form of 'که از' meaning 'that/from which' and introduces the reason or cause clause. گردش آسمان is a genitive phrase 'the turning/rotation of the heavens' (گردش governing آسمان). The clause sets up a cosmological reason.
segments.s461-g48: نیابد is the negative 3rd-person form 'does not find / will not find' (negated form of یابد). گذر 'passage/passing' is the object 'a passage/escape'. مرد نیکی‌گمان is a compact noun phrase 'a man of good expectation/hope' (the ezāfe is omitted poetically). The whole segment says that such a hopeful/virtuous man does not find passage according to the heavens' motion.
segments.s461-g17: چنان دان is an imperative/commandal expression meaning 'consider it so' or 'regard (yourself) thus' (چنان = 'so/thus', دان = 'know/consider'). که introduces the content clause that follows ('that / that you are...'). نادان‌ترین is the superlative adjective 'most ignorant'. کس here means 'person' or 'anyone'. توی in this line functions archaically (close to 'تویی' = 'you are' or 'in you'), not the spatial 'in/among', so its standard lexical senses do not capture its use here.
segments.s461-g18: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. پند is the noun 'advice, admonition'. دانندگان is the plural agentive 'those who know' = 'the learned/wise'. نْشُنو‌ی (نشنوی) is the present-subjunctive/negative-able form of شنیدن used here with a sense 'do not hear / do not heed' (second-person). Together the clause means 'if you do not heed the advice of the learned.'
segments.s461-g19: ز here is the preposition 'from' (poetic/stylistic). خاکیم is a nominal+copula form 'we are dust' (خاک + -یم). و هم = 'and also' or 'and likewise'. خاک را زاده‌ایم is an emphatic/idiomatic expression 'we have been born of dust' (را as a definite/partitive marker on خاک, زاده‌ایم = 'we have been born / are born of').
segments.s461-g20: به introduces the beneficiary/target 'to/into'. بیچارگی is a noun 'misery/helplessness'. دل is 'heart'. بدو is a fused short form 'to it / to him' (here 'to misery'). داده‌ایم is perfective 'we have given' (دادن + perfective plural suffix), so the whole clause reads 'we have given our heart to misery.'
segments.s461-g21: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. نیک is the adjective 'good/virtuous'. باشی is the present-subjunctive/optative form of 'to be' here 'you are / you be'. بماندت is an archaic 3rd-person-future/preservative form 'will remain/shall remain' (the clitic -ت marks the 2nd/3rd-person in older usage), and نام is 'name' in the sense of 'reputation/fame'. Together: 'If you are virtuous, your name will remain.'
segments.s461-g22: به is the preposition 'to/at'. تخت is 'throne'. کیی‌بر is a proper name (a kingly name) marking whose throne is meant. بوی is the noun 'scent/air' used metaphorically here (literally 'a scent of'), and شادکام is an adjective 'fortunate, joyful'. The phrase as a whole places the enduring name upon Kiyībar's throne, with the image of a 'scent/air of felicity' — a poetic attribute.
segments.s461-g23: وگر is a conditional 'but if'. بد is the adjective 'bad/evil' and کنی is the present-subjunctive/optative of 'to do' (here 'you do'). جز means 'except' or 'other than'. بدی is the noun 'evil/harm'. ندروی is a verb form with the negative prefix ن- plus a root meaning 'to obtain/reap' (classical/poetic morphology); here it functions 'you will obtain/reap nothing but (evil)'. The clause therefore says 'if you do evil, you will get nothing but evil.'
segments.s461-g24: شبی is an indefinite temporal noun 'one night' or 'on some night'. در = 'in' and جهان = 'the world / this life'. شادمان is an adjective 'happy/cheerful'. نغنوی is a negative-flected form (ن- prefix + غنود 'to sleep' / 'to be at ease') expressing 'you will not sleep/be happy' — so the line says 'one night in the world you will not sleep rejoicing' (i.e., you will not have a single happy night).
segments.s461-g1: This clause is a compact, poetic order: بمرد (verb) before the locative اندرَان and temporal چند گه. بمرد is a past-tense/optative form meaning ‘died’ (here used narratively). اندرَان = ‘among them’ is a prepositional/locative adverb placed after the verb for metrical flow. چند گه (چند + گه) gives the temporal sense ‘on several occasions / at one time’ and modifies the whole clause; the name فیلقوس follows as the subject/name being referred to, in inverted word order typical of epic verse.
segments.s461-g2: This is a short verbal clause with the prepositional phrase به روم اندرون ‘into / in Rome, inside’ preceding the copula بود ‘was’ (past). یک‌چند functions adverbially (‘for a short while / some time’) and sits before the final item بوس, which here is uncertain or elliptical in sense (the manuscript reading is terse). The overall structure is location → copula → temporal adverbial, a common paratactic ordering in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s461-g3: سکندر is the subject (proper name). The preposition به + تخت نیا gives the goal/place ‘to the (ancestral) throne’ (تخت = throne; نیا here denotes ancestral/forefather’s, a classical epithet). برنشست is a prefixed past verb (بر- + نشست) meaning ‘sat upon / mounted’ and comes at the end as the main finite verb, following Persian poetic word order where verb often closes the clause.
segments.s461-g4: بهی جست pairs an adverbial/qualifying word بهی (‘well’/‘nobly’ or an intensifier) with the simple past verb جست ‘he leapt’ (jūst). The coordinating و connects the second clause, in which دست بدی را is the definite object phrase: دست = ‘hand’, بدی = ‘evil / wicked (one)’ so دست بدی = ‘the wicked hand’. را marks it as the definite object, and ببست (he bound/tied) is the final past verb. Note the verb-final structure and the use of را to mark the object.
segments.s461-g5: یکی نامداری is an idiomatic phrase meaning ‘a certain notable / a man of renown’ (یکی = ‘a certain’, نامداری = ‘renown / notable one’). The short particle/word بد here is archaic/elliptical in function in the manuscript (not the usual ‘bad’ meaning) and appears before آنگه ‘then’; the sequence ends with the goal phrase به روم ‘to Rome’. The clause omits an explicit verb (a frequent poetic compression) and is understood as ‘a certain renowned man (went) then to Rome.’
segments.s461-g6: کزو = کِه + ز + و (contracted) or the fused relative ‘from whom / from which’. شاد is an adjective ‘glad / joyful’. Again بد appears in an archaic/grammaticalized role in the manuscript and does not carry the ordinary modern sense ‘bad’ here. آن همه مرز و بوم = ‘that entire border and land / the whole realm’ is the extended domain affected; the clause means ‘from him joy (came) to that whole realm’ with the verb (came/was) left implicit, a typical poetic ellipsis.
segments.s461-g7: حکیمی ‘a sage’ is the head noun, followed by the relative particle که ‘who/that’. The short word بد before ارسطالیس in the manuscript functions idiomatically as ‘called / named’ (an archaic usage not present in the provided modern senses), so بد ارسطالیس نام = ‘named Aristotle’. نام here follows as a noun ‘name / called’. This is a relative clause identifying the sage.
segments.s461-g8: This is a simple adjectival list modifying حکیم: خردمند ‘wise’, و ‘and’, بیدار ‘awake/alert’, و ‘and’, گسترده ‘broad/expansive’ modifying the final noun کام ‘desire / appetite / satisfaction’. The adjectives are simple predicates (attributive) stacked by و; there is no finite verb, a typical nominative/elliptical descriptive construction in epic verse.

sentences.s461-s45: He drew the army up around the water’s edge; because of their cuirasses, no one saw the sea’s water.
sentences.s461-s37: Golden belts and gold‑adorned sashes — that same Indian sword in a golden scabbard.
sentences.s461-s38: So many brocades and dinars were given that he could not bear the burden of the requested (gifts).
sentences.s461-s39: Many a warning came: horsemen — great warriors and famed fighters.
sentences.s461-s40: From that place Iran took up the post; it seized the hearts of lions and the harp (hearts) of the brave.
sentences.s461-s13: Goodness lay within the king’s reach; on the world's ill days no one was found.
sentences.s461-s14: Alexander heard this and it pleased him; by command a spokesman came to him.
sentences.s461-s15: At his command he did the deed that he did, in feasting and in battle and in shame and in combat.
sentences.s461-s16: He struck anew at every occasion; when he went to the throne he established himself upon it.
sentences.s461-s29: Out stepped that famed sovereign — a lamb-bearer — with such a renowned army.
sentences.s461-s30: Behind the Roman commander there was a banner; upon its red and turquoise field an inscription was placed.
sentences.s461-s31: On the Homa's reed staff there was an inscription; on it was written, 'lover of the Cross.'
sentences.s461-s32: To Egypt there came from Rome so many troops that they closed the road against ants and gnats.
sentences.s461-s33: The two armies came face to face and remained hostile for a week.
sentences.s461-s34: On the eighth day defeat came into Egypt, and Alexander blocked their road.
sentences.s461-s35: They were so trapped on a single road that the captor's hand was left idle.
sentences.s461-s36: From cowherds and from horses and from the برگستوان, from sleepers and from Indian daggers.
sentences.s461-s41: When Dārā heard that an army from Rome had moved and had come to this frontier and homeland.
sentences.s461-s42: They set out from Istakhr with so many troops that they closed off the road with spears.
sentences.s461-s43: He was intent on Rome from Pars, intending to pass from Iran into the prosperous land.
sentences.s461-s44: When he brought the army before the Euphrates, the host's numbers were greater than vegetation.
sentences.s461-s5: That pure‑minded man went before Alexander, spoke eloquently, and took his place.
sentences.s461-s6: He said to him, 'O chief, fortunate one, you will surely lose your renown in this affair.'
sentences.s461-s7: For the throne of the Kayanids has seen many like you; it will not remain (long) with anyone.
sentences.s461-s8: Whenever — as you say — I have reached this place, the world should not be my guide.
sentences.s461-s25: I must relinquish my place in the world; the bad and the good must be reckoned.
sentences.s461-s26: You must now make your heart leave the homeland and give it up to rest.
sentences.s461-s27: He opened the lids of the ancestral treasuries and commanded that his army be made ready.
sentences.s461-s28: At nightfall he rose; from Rome there arose an uproar — from the city and from the court of the new commander.
sentences.s461-s17: It so happened that one day a messenger — eloquent, clear‑hearted and noble — (appeared).
sentences.s461-s18: From nearby, Dārā came to Rome; where will a cry come from the prosperous homeland?
sentences.s461-s19: Before Alexander he spoke that speech; Alexander became sorrowful because of the ancient cry.
sentences.s461-s20: He said to him, 'Go and tell Dārā that from our cry there has now arisen color and scent.'
sentences.s461-s21: That a bird which made its testicles golden for the man, and made the head of the cry worthless.
sentences.s461-s22: He sent an answer; when he heard that, he became frightened and vanished from Rum.
sentences.s461-s23: Alexander summoned the whole army, and he recited the tale of the past before them.
sentences.s461-s24: He said thus that, from the turning of the heavens, a man of hopeful/good expectation will not find a passage.
sentences.s461-s9: Consider yourself as if you were the most ignorant of people if you will not listen to the advice of the learned.
sentences.s461-s10: We are of dust, and likewise we were born from dust; we have given our heart to misery.
sentences.s461-s11: If you are virtuous, your name will remain; (it will remain) on the throne of Kiyībar as a sign/scent of good fortune.
sentences.s461-s12: But if you do evil, you will reap nothing but evil; one night in this world you will not sleep gladly.
sentences.s461-s1: At one time Filqūs died among them; for a short while he was inside Rome.
sentences.s461-s2: Alexander mounted the ancestral throne; he leapt forward and bound the wicked hand.
sentences.s461-s3: Then a certain man of renown went to Rome; from him there came joy to that whole realm and land.
sentences.s461-s4: A sage who was called Aristotle, wise and alert and of wide desire.
